entry.title: Car & Conveyance IT583 (ST:09 - Availing OYCS)
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

Please help me to resolve issue on IT583 Car and Conveyance exemption & Perks for sub type 09 Availing OYCS (Indian Payroll).

I have requirement as shared below

Wage Type

Conveyance Type

Maint by

Electric Car/CC

Ex.Monthly

5S05

Availing OYCS

Employer

Within 1.6

₹   5,000.00

 

Above 1.6

₹   7,000.00 


Configuration

  • I had maintained V_T7INC1,C3,C5,C9 ; V_T7INA9,A3 and V_T7INT9
  • Wage Type IT0015 - 5S05 (Reimbursement)

Result Log 

  • For Availing OYCS (Lesser than equal 1.6 CC) >>>>> I got /130 and /4E3 - Rs.60000 --- from table V_T7INT9 Exemption monthly is Rs.5000. So, 5000*12 = 60000 in /130 (Annual Exempt)
  • For Availing OYCS (Above 1.6 CC) >>>>> Same Rs.60000 I am getting reflected in /130 wage type in RT. which is not the expected result. 

Here is my question:

  1. How can I able to give 2 exemptions for same wage type as shown in above table?
  2. How can I able to provide exempt monthly on car CC (Above or below 1.6) in IT583? 

Please provide me a solution or PCR to resolve the issue. 

*Note - Perks value is calculated as per the V_T7INC5 table and /3c1, /3c5 reflecting in RT for Availing OYCS (Less than equal 1.6 CC).

entry.title: Re: MRP Run for FG Material Considers Supply and Demand f...
entry.content:
Good day @GEABGR 

Thank you for your question.

Your requirement has two separate aspects:

  1. Run MRP only for one selected WBS element: The system supports this when the material is of transaction MD51.
  2. Keep supply and demand separated by WBS element: This is supported when you use individual project stock to plan the material.

The Schedule MRP Runs, allows the planning scope to be restricted by criteria such as plant, MRP area, material, product group, and MRP controller. It does not provide a WBS element as a planning scope selection. Therefore, an MRP run for the material may process all relevant project segments for that material.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/3e467f54737c8c4ce10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

However, processing multiple WBS segments in one run does not mean that their quantities should be netted together. Note that stock is assigned exclusively to a particular WBS element and cannot be used for another WBS element or anonymous stock.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4032610758dc437089f0c28320eec93f/af08789e7a6842f68acb056a0bfede9a.html?locale=en-US

I recommend you check the following:

In Manage Product Master Data app, select the finished material and relevant plant.               

In Plants, check the MRP Data, check the individual/Collective Requirements setting. For project specific planning, the material should not be configured as Collective requirements only.                           

Repeat this check for relevant BOM components because a component configured for collective requirements can be planned in the common net segment.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4032610758dc437089f0c28320eec93f/1427c273802f4052a64933db7d7bdf9a.html?locale=en-US

After running MRP, open Manage Material Coverage, enter the product and plant, and examine the individual project segments.

If quantities from another WBS are genuinely covering the selected project segment despite correct individual requirements settings, then my suggestion is to raise a case with SAP. Ensure you provide your MRP job log and necessary details.

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration

Part 2 of a 4-part beginner series on SAP Event Mesh.

A quick recap

In Part 1, we covered the "why" and the "what" — Event-Driven Architecture, the publish-subscribe model, and the three building blocks of SAP Event Mesh: topics, queues, and namespaces. We then provisioned an Event Mesh instance on SAP BTP (region AP11), created queues for each consumer system, subscribed those queues to topics, and generated a service key holding our connection credentials.

👉If you haven't read it yet, start with Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics & Queues — this post builds directly on that setup. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-event-driven-architecture-topics-queues/bc-p/14428494#M179525 

So we have a working Event Mesh instance. But how do we actually send a message into it, and how do we get SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) to publish and consume events? That's what we'll do here.

We'll work in two stages:

  1. Validate the setup with Postman — prove that authentication works and that we can publish a message before we touch CPI.
  2. Build the CPI iFlows — one to publish events over HTTP, and one to consume them over AMQP.

Here's the picture we're building toward:

 

 

Let's get started.

3. Testing with Postman

Why test with Postman first? Because if something goes wrong later in CPI, we want to know the Event Mesh side is already working. Postman lets us isolate the problem. It's a habit worth building.

Step 1 — Configure OAuth2 authentication

Open Postman, create a new request, and go to the Authorization tab. We'll set it up like this:

Field Value
Auth TypeOAuth 2.0
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Access Token URLhttps://integrationsuite-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Scope(leave empty)
Client AuthenticationSend client credentials in body

 

 

Now here's the single most common mistake people make, and it's worth pausing on:

⚠️Watch out: The Event Mesh Client ID contains special characters such as | and !. If we send the credentials as a Basic Auth header, those characters get corrupted during encoding, and we get a confusing invalid_client: Bad credentials error — even though our credentials are perfectly correct. The fix is to choose "Send client credentials in body." Always.

Click Get New Access Token. If everything is correct, Postman returns a token. We're authenticated. 🎉

Step 2 — Publish a message to a topic

Now let's send our first event. We use the messaging REST endpoint from the service key:

 
 
POST https://integrationsuite{}.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com     /messagingrest/v1/topics/{encoded-topic-name}/messages

Notice {encoded-topic-name}. This is where beginners lose an hour, so let's be careful.

The URL-encoding rule:

⚠️Every / in the topic name must be encoded as %2F — not %2. The trailing F is required. Leave it off and you'll get a 404 or, worse, silent wrong-topic routing.

So our topic name transforms like this:

  Value
Topic name (plain)S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster
Topic name (URL-encoded)S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster

Which gives us the full URL:

 
 
https://integrationsuite {}-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages

Next, set the headers:

Header Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

A quick note on x-qos (quality of service): 0 means at most once (fire and forget — fast, but no delivery guarantee), and 1 means at least once (guaranteed delivery). For our test, 0 is fine.

Finally, the request body — a simple CloudEvents-style payload:

 
 
json
{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "S4HC.Testing.Masterdata.Shopify.CustomerMaster",
  "source": "/S4HC/Testing/Masterdata",
  "data": {
    "CustomerID": "C001",
    "Name": "Test Customer"
  }
}

 

Hit Send. And now for the part that surprises everyone:

✅A successful publish returns HTTP 204 No Content. There's no response body, and that's correct. It does not mean something went wrong — it means the message was accepted and is now sitting in the queue, waiting for a consumer.

Step 3 — Verify the message arrived

Let's confirm. Go to the Event Mesh UI → Queues, click the queue you subscribed (for example S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify), and check the message count — it should have gone up by one.

 

That's it — we've published an event to Event Mesh and confirmed it landed in the right queue, all without touching CPI yet. Now we know the foundation is solid.

4. CPI iFlow Configuration

Time to bring CPI into the picture. We'll set up the shared credential once, then build the publish and consume sides.

4.1 Create the OAuth2 credential in Security Material

Both of our iFlows will authenticate the same way, so we create the credential once and reuse it.

In CPI, go to Monitor → Security Material → Add, and select OAuth2 Client Credentials:

Field Value
Credential NameEventMeshPOC (remember this name — we'll reference it in the adapters)
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Token Service URLhttps://integration-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Send BodyYes

 

⚠️Same gotcha as Postman: make sure Send Body is set to Yes. The special characters in the Client ID will break Basic Auth.

4.2 Publishing messages — use the HTTP adapter

Here's an important design decision, and it catches a lot of people:

⚠️Known CPI behaviour: The AMQP adapter automatically lowercases topic and queue names. So CustomerMaster silently becomes customermaster, the topic doesn't match, and we get amqp:not-allowed (error 220041). To publish, we sidestep this entirely by using the HTTP Receiver adapter instead, which preserves the exact case.

In your publisher iFlow, add an HTTP Receiver adapter:

Connection tab

Field Value
URLhttps://enterprise-messaging-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages
MethodPOST
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC
Content Typeapplication/json

Then, just before the HTTP adapter, add a Content Modifier step to set the required headers:

Header Name Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

 

That's the publish side done. When this iFlow runs, it POSTs the payload to the topic exactly the way our Postman test did — and we already know that works.

4.3 Consuming messages — use the AMQP adapter

When CPI is the consumer (the subscriber listening for events), we use the AMQP Sender adapter. And here's the key: we consume from a Queue, not a Topic — which conveniently avoids the lowercase issue we hit on the publish side.

AMQP Connection tab

Field Value
Hostenterprise-messaging-messaging-gateway.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com
Port443
Path/protocols/amqp10ws
Proxy TypeInternet
Connect with TLSChecked
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC

⚠️The Host field should contain only the hostname — no wss:// prefix. The protocol path goes in the Path field separately (/protocols/amqp10ws). Mixing them up gives errors like wss: Name or service not known or a failed WebSocket handshake.

AMQP Processing tab

Field Value
Destination TypeQueue
Queue NameS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify
DeliveryPersistent

 

✅Tip: Using Queue as the destination type (rather than Topic) on the consume side keeps the exact name intact. Reading from a queue does not have the lowercase problem that publishing to a topic does.

Putting it together

Let's step back and look at what we've built:

  • We validated authentication and publishing with Postman, so we knew the Event Mesh side worked before writing a single iFlow.
  • On the publish side, our CPI iFlow uses the HTTP Receiver adapter to POST events to a topic — preserving case and avoiding the AMQP lowercase trap.
  • On the consume side, a second iFlow uses the AMQP Sender adapter pointed at a Queue to pull events as they arrive.
  • Both adapters share a single OAuth2 Client-Credentials credential, sent in the body.

The two recurring themes — case sensitivity and credentials in the body — are exactly the things that cause the most head-scratching for beginners, so keep them front of mind.

What's next

We now have a complete round trip: events flowing from CPI into Event Mesh and back out again. But the first time you wire this up, something will break — that's normal, and it's where real learning happens.

In Part 3 — Troubleshooting & Quick Reference, we'll go through the exact errors you're most likely to hit (invalid_client, amqp:not-allowed, the %2F trap, WebSocket handshake failures), what causes each one, and how to fix it — plus a one-page cheat sheet of every URL and value you need.

If this helped, a like or a comment is always appreciated — and feel free to share what you're integrating with Event Mesh. See you in Part 3!

 

Primary Tag: SAP Integration Suite Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Cloud Integration, SAP CPI, AMQP, OAuth2, Postman, SAP BTP, Event-Driven Architecture

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-07-06
entry.content:
Hey SAP Community!

I'm excited to share What's New in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM  Cloud UM).

In this post, I'll cover the latest features, improvements, and enhancements, and show you how they can help you get even more out of the solution.

Manage Readers

Configurable Request Timeout for Reader Functions

Reader functions pull data from model-based sources like standalone Model Entities, Model HANA, or SAC Model Connections. If you want to learn more about how SAC Model Connections work, check out the related blog post: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model: Pushing and Pulling data to and from SAP Analytics Cloud.

Reader functions that use OData Service or SAC Model connections now have a new Request Timeout field. This lets you control how long the system waits for a response from an external service before giving up on the request.

This is especially useful when working with large datasets that take longer to load — instead of the request failing, you can simply set a longer wait time to make sure the data comes through successfully. It gives you more flexibility and control over how your system handles slower or heavier queries.

How to set the Request Timeout 

  1. In Reader function → General Information, choose Edit. While in edit mode, select an OData Service or SAC Model connection. The Request Timeout field becomes available
  2. Enter a value in seconds, or
  3. Leave it empty to use the system default (60 seconds). 

Note: The Request Timeout field appears only for OData Service and SAC Model connection types. It is not shown for other connection types. 

Allowed values 

  • Leave Request Timeout empty- keeps the system default timeout of 60 seconds. 
  • Enter a value between 1 - 300 seconds to override the default value.

  • Values outside the 1–300 range are not accepted. A validation message appears when saving.

If the Request Timeout is set too short and the service takes longer to respond, the Reader function will stop at that cutoff point and show an error message letting you know the run failed. 

If you have the EVENTLOG, EVENTLOG_READ, or SYSTEM_ALL role, you can check the details in Manage Event Logs application, where you'll see a message indicating that the remote service took longer than the configured timeout (shown in milliseconds).

To extend the timeout, increase the Request Timeout value (up to the 300-second maximum) and run the function again. You can confirm it worked by going to Manage Event Logs application and checking that the corresponding "runFunction" event shows a state of "OK”. 

If the Reader function keeps timing out because of large datasets, another option is to adjust the Rows per Request setting. By reducing how many rows are fetched at a time, the workload gets split into smaller, more manageable chunks — which can help prevent timeouts from happening in the first place. For more details, check out the Reader section in the Application Help for Universal Model.

Manage Event Logs

Enhanced Traceability in Event Logs: Message Details Field 

The Event Log now has a Message Details field that shows the primary key of each message created during SOAP service writer operations. This makes it easy to trace log entries directly back to their corresponding data records.

This enhancement brings several key advantages:

  • End-to-end traceability — Every message ID in the Event Log is now directly linked to its source record through the UUID shown in the Message Details field.
  • Precise record lookup — When processing large volumes of data, you can quickly pinpoint specific rows or documents to their corresponding Event Log entries without having to cross-reference manually.
  • Reliable auditability — The primary key in Message Details gives you a solid reference point for tracking data throughout SOAP service writer operations.

When a SOAP service writer processes large datasets, the Message Details field makes it easy to match any Event Log entry to its exact source record, speeding up data validation and investigation.

Data Screen

Improved Data Export

The Export All Data feature has been improved to handle exporting complete datasets, no matter how large. You can now export all available records directly from the application — whether that's thousands or hundreds of thousands of entries — giving you more flexibility and easier access to your data.

Here's what's new with this improvement:

  • Full dataset export — the Export All Data option now processes your complete dataset. 
  • CSV files are now provided in a compressed (zipped) format to reduce file size, making downloads faster. 
  • XLSX format exports are provided as standard .xlsx files. 
  • Export performance is optimized for large datasets. 

 To export all data using the Export All Data option: 

  1. Navigate to the Data Screen and open the grid view containing the data you want to export. 
  2. Choose the Export All Data option from the grid menu. 
  3. A progress indicator is displayed while the file is being generated. 
  4. Once complete, the exported file is available for download. CSV exports are provided as a zipped archive; XLSXexports are provided as standard .xlsx files. 

Joule

Extended Capabilities (File, Image, Web Search, App Help) 

Joule just got a lot smarter when it comes to understanding context. With four new input capabilities — file uploads, image and vision support, web search, and built-in application help — you can now bring external information directly into your Joule conversation and get more accurate, relevant answers.

You can now attach documents to your Joule conversation and ask questions directly about their content. Choose the paperclip icon in the message input to attach a file. Supported formats include PDF, DOCX, JPEG, PNG, and WEBP.

Once a file is uploaded, Joule can search through its content and pull out the most relevant passages. The results appear as citations in the Sources panel, complete with the filename and page reference so you always know exactly where the information came from.

Files from the recent messages remain searchable in follow-up messages. 

Joule can now see and interpret images that you paste or drop directly into the chat. Simply drag and drop or paste an image into the message input — you'll see a thumbnail preview before sending. Supported formats include JPEG, PNG, and WEBP.

Joule can describe and reason about visual content — for example, you can share a screenshot of a report or chart and ask Joule to explain what it shows. You can include up to 5 images per message.

When configured for the tenant, Joule gains access to live web results — enabling answers that go beyond internal PaPM data. You will know web search is available by the globe icon that appears in the input footer.

Turn on Web Search to allow Joule to retrieve live web results – useful for questions about recent releases, external documentation, or general SAP topics. Web search results appear as content citations with title and URL (opens in new tab). 

Note: If web search is not configured for the tenant, the globe icon is not visible.

Joule now comes with built-in PaPM domain knowledge, available in both Ask Mode and Act Mode, so it can answer conceptual and how-to questions without needing to query live data. You can ask about available function types, how allocation works, team roles and permissions, or any other PaPM concept — and Joule will give you an answer based on its built-in application knowledge. Responses from application help appear as content citations in the Sources panel.

With these four new capabilities, Joule becomes an even more powerful context-aware assistant. Whether you need to analyze an uploaded document, interpret a chart, catch up on the latest product news, or get a quick explanation of a PaPM concept — it's all available within a single conversation.

More details are available in the SAP Help Portal.  

Multi-Step Workflows (Act Mode Skills) 

Running complex PaPM operations just got a lot easier. With the introduction of Act Mode Skills, Joule can now guide you through multi-step workflows automatically — all you need to do is describe what you want in plain language.

Behind the scenes, Joule follows built-in step-by-step recipes called "skills" and runs all the required operations in the right order. You see the result — Joule handles the complexity.

In Act Mode, you can perform the following actions on the Available Workflow Categories:

Report Workflows

  • Create a full report:  including pages, page elements, and report tabs — by describing the goal in a single message. Joule handles the entire creation sequence and activates the report automatically. 
  • Add a page to a report: you can extend an existing report by adding new pages without opening the report editor. 

Process Workflows

  • Create a process: you can create a process with multiple activities and the links between them by describing the desired structure. Joule sets up all components in the correct order. 

  • Activate a process: you can run a full end-to-end process — Joule activates the process, runs all activities, handles the confirm/reject loop, and marks the process as complete. 

Environment & Function Workflows 

  • Activate an environment: Joule verifies the required functions, initiates the activation, and confirms the status — without you needing to navigate to the environment screen. 

  • Deactivate an environment: Joule asks which cleanup options to apply and then proceeds with deactivation based on your choice. 
  • Create a function: You can create and configure functions of various types – such as allocation, model entity, or calculation – in a specific environment through a single natural language request.  

  • Run a function: you can run a function and receive a status update directly in the chat.

Team Workflow 

  • Create a team: you can create a new team for a specific environment and optionally assign reader or writer access — all in one step.

Act Mode Skills are a major step forward for PaPM productivity.  Complex PaPM operations can now be completed with a single, plain language request — Joule takes care of the rest.

For more details, visit the SAP Help Portal.

Act Mode & Safety Controls 

Joule in SAP PaPM now supports Act Mode — a read-write mode that lets you create, update, and delete PaPM entities directly through natural language directly through natural language, from a single conversational user interface. Act Mode makes it easy to perform efficient and controlled PaPM operations straight from the chat.

With the new Ask/Act toggle in the Joule panel, switching to Act Mode and performing write operations is as simple as flipping a switch.

In Act Mode, users can: 

  • Create entities: users can create new PaPM entities such as environments, functions, processes, or teams by describing them in natural language. 
  • Update entities: users can modify the properties of existing entities — such as names, descriptions, or configurations — without opening the entity screen. 
  • Delete entities: users can remove entities they no longer need directly through the chat. 
  • Initiate actions: users can initiate operations such as activating or deactivating environments and running functions directly through chat. 
  • All read operations from Ask Mode also work in Act Mode. 

 To protect you from accidental data loss, Joule now includes configurable confirmation prompts for destructive and sensitive operations.

Auto Approval Toggle 

You can find this option in the Joule panel by choosing the Settings icon. By default, it's turned on for new users, meaning all operations run without confirmation. When turned off, a confirmation dialog will appear before any sensitive or destructive operation is carried out.

Note: Certain irreversible operations – such as delete, erase data, remove environment, and anonymize user – always require confirmation, regardless of the Auto Approval setting. 

Confirmation Dialog 

The confirmation dialog shows the operation type, entity name, and a warning message. When applicable, it also includes a Verify link that lets you navigate to the entity screen before making a decision. From there, you can either Approve to complete the operation or Decline to cancel it.

The confirmation dialog only appears for sensitive operations — not for simple create or update actions. This includes operations such as delete, activate, deactivate, run, erase data, erase rules, remove environment, lock/unlock data, abort/reset/complete process, confirm/reject activity, and anonymize user, among others.

When the system associates an event log with an operation, an Event Logs button appears directly in the Joule chat response. Choosing it opens the Manage Event Logs screen in a new tab, already filtered to the relevant log entry — making it easy to audit and trace every AI-driven change.

Note: The Event Logs button appears only when the system associates an event log with the operation. Not all operations generate event logs. 

For more details, visit the SAP Help Portal.

Want to catch up on previous updates? Browse our earlier What's New blog posts under the #PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag to see what you may have missed.

That’s all for this edition. See you in the next What's New blog post!

entry.title: SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 is coming: General Availability as of 16th of July 2026
entry.content:
Hello Community,

On 16th of July 2026 official support for our new SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 will start and you will be able to download the software from SAP Support Portal. In this article you can find the latest information on the new release (features, lifecycle,...). Concerning the new features, this article is an extension of the article we published for our Beta Program.

Let's start with the basic facts and get to the long and really exciting list of features later.

SAP GUI for Windows Lifecycle

SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 was created with the latest Microsoft Visual Studio 2026. However, due to the changed lifecycle management for Visual Studio by Microsoft (see also: https://learn.microsoft.com/de-de/lifecycle/products/visual-studio-2026) we decided to decouple the lifecycle of SAP GUI for Windows from Visual Studio starting with release 8.10. We are aware that you expect a reasonable lifetime for a new SAP GUI for Windows release and have therefore decided to support SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 until 30th of June 2029.

The chart below shows the current (6th of July 2026) lifecycle information of SAP GUI for Windows releases 7.70 up to 8.10. For the latest information always refer to SAP Note 147519.

Platform Support in SAP GUI for Windows 8.10

At the time of writing SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 supports the same platforms that 8.00 supports:

  • Windows 11
  • Windows Server 2016
  • Windows Server 2019
  • Windows Server 2022
  • Windows Server 2025

For the latest information see SAP Note 66971.

Please consider that Windows Server 2016 will soon be out of support by Microsoft: https://learn.microsoft.com/de-de/lifecycle/products/windows-server-2016. Therefore, we cannot recommend using this platform anymore when rolling out SAP GUI for Windows 8.10.

Now and finally the most interesting part: What is new in SAP GUI for Windows 8.10?

New Features in SAP GUI for Windows 8.10

Below, I just list the new features which are part of the initial delivery (patchlevel 0). As patches are delivered and small functional extensions are added, we will keep SAP Note 3549180 updated, so refer to that SAP Note for the latest information. You can find the exact same information provided below also in the release notes document that ships with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10.
Please note that links leading to our documentation will only show the latest and updated documents once 8.10 has been shipped.

You might ask yourself why the delivery of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 was postponed from January 2026. There are two main reasons for that:

  • Honestly speaking, the release was not acceptable from a quality perspective. In the meanwhile, we invested a lot of time and energy into fixing all issues and have managed to provide a good and stable new release. Also, the performance was improved in many areas.
  • Much more exciting is the second reason. We invested a lot of time into a close collaboration with one of our customers. The idea of this project was to optimize a complex manual process and replace it by a semi-automated process powered by SAP Joule, SAP Business Client and SAP GUI for Windows. During this project we created the foundations for integration scenarios like this and proved that substantial benefits can be generated with limited investment. Now, with the help of the “Frontend Actions”, an interaction between the application and SAP Joule is possible while the user stays in full control of what happens - the application is still running in SAP GUI for Windows while the process takes over the data extraction and the automatic population of the input into the elements of the application running in SAP GUI.
    More information on this topic will be published in a separate blog - stay tuned.

Horizon Theme Family

The latest addition to the themes available in SAP GUI for Windows is the successor to the Quartz theme family. The Horizon themes offer a visual integration into the current Fiori 3.0 themes available in other SAP UI frameworks and introduce a state-of-the-art visual appearance:

The feature set of the Horizon themes is quite similar to the one from previous theme families with some visual improvements that boost usability. The Horizon themes are available for all SAP products still being supported, are released for Accessibility and support the Fiori features (right aligned labels, automatic usage of the full screen width and so forth). They are the default themes as of release 8.10.

Improvements include:

  • A much stronger focus visualization compared to previous themes
  • A better differentiation between read-only and editable elements (also in table structures)
  • When dialog boxes are displayed, the main window is displayed differently indicating that it is not active and drawing the visual focus to the dialog box
  • Completely reworked and more structured display of tabstrips
  • About 30-40 icons used by applications running in SAP GUI for Windows have regained more distinct colors. And we keep working on this area. Hopefully, we will be able to share more good news related to icons and their colors in a couple of months.

Theme Selection

Automatic theme selection based on the Windows theme

The theme selection combobox in the SAP GUI options dialog contains a new value called “Match Microsoft Windows theme”. When this option selected, upon starting, SAP GUI will automatically use a Horizon theme that suits the currently used Windows theme (light theme, dark theme, high contrast black theme, high contrast white theme).

User theme selection override

Previously, an administrator could not override the theme selection a user might have made previously, because in SAP GUI for Windows user settings have higher priority than administrator settings. This made the introduction of a new default theme more challenging.

As of release 8.10, it is possible to override the user selection by defining the following registry value under

HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\SAP\General\Appearance (64bit version)
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\SAP\General\Appearance (32bit version)

InitialDefaultThemeOverride (REG_DWORD)

You can assign an integer to this registry value. The concrete number provided specifies how often SAP GUI shall remove a user theme selection when starting. Typically, this is required only a single time (value = 1). When the override is performed, the theme selection done by the user is deleted and thus a theme set by the administrator under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE or the SAP GUI default theme will be used.

Custom Theming for Horizon Themes

As of release 8.10, SAP GUI for Windows supports custom theming when using a Horizon theme.
With this feature, SAP GUI for Windows can be displayed in themes created by an administrator / user interface designer / user based on the official Horizon themes provided by SAP. This feature enhances the theming capabilities of SAP GUI for Windows by far.

Use cases for using self-developed themes include:

  • Providing corporate identify (colors, branding image)
  • Improving certain aspects of the UI, because you have special visual requirements (like color blindness)
  • Distinguishing one SAP system from other SAP systems by the look of SAP GUI connected to those systems
  • Improving individual aspects of the official SAP theme that you might not like

You can change almost all colors in SAP GUI for Windows, and you can of course also change the branding image. A theme designed by you could look like this (just a sample for demo purposes):

The process for custom theming ideally looks like this:

  • A UI designer creates a theming concept which suites your needs
  • The design is implemented by creating a custom theme via SAP UI Theme Designer
  • The theme is exported from SAP UI Theme Designer with all information needed by SAP GUI for Windows
  • The theme is tested, refined and once found suitable, it is deployed to a web server / file share
  • The administrator defines a theming configuration file which references the new theme
  • The administrator deploys a registry value to the client PCs which references the theming configuration
  • The new theme can be used, or it can even be predefined as the default theme

Quick Theming

Apart from the full theming process outlined above, SAP GUI for Windows now also supports a “Quick Theming” which can be done very easily via a page in SAP GUI options dialog. Here, you can change 6 of the most important colors to influence the look of the SAP GUI window. However, since you can change only 6 colors, this approach is limited in terms of what you can achieve.

SAP Logon (Pad)

Extended filter functionality

In previous versions of SAP GUI for Windows the filter field in SAP Logon (Pad) could only be used to filter items (connections, SAP shortcuts, favorites) within the same folder. This is not what user who are using a nested tree structure need, because the typical use case is that you want to locate a connection that you know exists somewhere in a larger structure.

With SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 the filter functionality was substantially improved. Now, the filter searches through the complete tree structure and it also takes the complete information belonging to an item into account (like comments). Furthermore, the filter supports logical operations:

  • OR operator: Like “2024 2023“ or “2024 |2023“ to filter results matching at least one of the terms (this operator is the default)
  • AND operator +: Like “Basis +Development” to filter results matching both terms
  • NOT operator: Like “2024 -Support” to filter results matching the first term, but not the second one

Drag & Drop of connections and favorites to the Windows Desktop

So far, SAP Logon only supported dragging and dropping of SAP Shortcuts to the Windows Desktop. Now, users can also drag and drop connections and favorites from SAP Logon to the Windows Desktop. When such an object is dropped, an SAP Shortcut referencing the respective connection is created and the SAP Shortcut creation dialog is shown so that the user can refine the SAP Shortcut as required.

Caching for configuration files

So far, only SAP UI Landscape files could be cached on the client PC to avoid disruption of SAP GUI usage when the network connection to the configuration files cannot be established or the central files got corrupted. As of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10, the following additional configuration files are cached by default as well:

  • Security Rules File: See SAP GUI Security Guide on SAP Help Portal
  • Browser Fallback Configuration File: See SAP GUI Administration Guide on SAP Help Portal
  • Custom Theming Configuration File and Custom Theme definitions: SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 supports custom theming using themes created by the SAP Theme Designer. Administrators can deploy such custom themes via different means, one of them being the distribution via a file share or HTTP(S) server like for the other configuration files. In these scenarios, the custom theming information is always downloaded from the server and stored locally. See SAP GUI Administration Guide on SAP Help Portal

Main Window

Erasing the contents upon session time out

The connection between SAP GUI for Windows and the SAP system is interrupted if the user remains inactive for a configurable time span (profile parameter rdisp/gui_auto_logout, see also SAP Note 27320). However, the SAP GUI window remains visible in this case to inform the user what happened. In some situations, it may be desired to hide the contents of the SAP GUI windows instead when this happens – even though the clear recommendation is of course never to leave your client PC unlocked when unattended.

If the removal of the contents is desired upon reaching the timeout, this can be achieved by setting a newly introduced registry value under either one of these branches:

  • [HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\SAP\General\Appearance]
  • [HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\SAP\General\Appearance] (64bit version of SAP GUI)
  • [HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\SAP\General\Appearance] (32bit version of SAP GUI)

The name of the value is
ObscureWindowOnAutoLogout [REG_DWORD]
and it needs to be set to 1.

Values under HKEY_CURRENT_USER have priority over values under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE.

Grouping SAP GUI windows by SAP System ID / client

The Windows taskbar can be configured to combine windows to groups (either generally or when the taskbar is full). So far, all SAP GUI windows were always combined with the SAP Logon window in a single group. However, it may be desired to be able to better identify groups of windows belonging together. Therefore, as of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10, it is possible to select how to group SAP GUI windows in the taskbar.

The options dialog allows to select how to group:

  • No grouping (default behavior, just like in previous releases)
  • Group by SAP System ID: All windows connected to the same SAP System ID are grouped together regardless of the client you are logged on to
  • Group by SAP System ID and Client: All windows connected to the same SAP System ID and the same client of that system are grouped together

You can activate the new feature via SAP GUI Options Dialog:

When activated, this may for example look like this:

Please note:

  • In the rare case that you run multiple SAP Systems with the same SID and you connect to these systems at the same time, these are grouped, too, because SAP GUI cannot distinguish such connections.
  • It is not possible to pin groups to the taskbar with the exception being the group containing SAP GUI

Icons for buttons displayed in overflow menus

When an application has many buttons displayed in the header bar or the SAP GUI main window is not very wide, it can happen that buttons disappear into the “More” menu (also called the “overflow”). Before release 8.10, these buttons were just displayed as plain menu entries without their icons making it harder for users to find the desired button. Now, these buttons are shown along with their icons in both overflow menus in the header of the main window as well as in the footer of dialog boxes:

Button for showing entered password

As an optional feature that can be activated via SAP GUI options dialog, it is now possible to display an entered password to avoid typing errors. On screens which are related to the logon process, a button appears next to the password field which can be used to display and correct the password if needed. This feature can also be activated via F4.

New frontend language: Malay (MS)

SAP GUI specific tests like in SAP Logon or in the SAP GUI options dialog can now be shown in language Malay (MS).

New sound scheme “Immersive”

A new sound scheme called “Immersive” especially suited for loud working conditions is available on the “Sound Settings” page in SAP GUI Options Dialog.

Features in ALV Grid Control

Improved visualization of symbols for IDA ALV

In the IDA ALV (see also https://help.sap.com/doc/saphelp_nw75/7.5.5/en-US/ef/eb734c8e6f41939c39fa15ce51eb4e/frameset.htm) mathematical symbols (totals, average, maximum and minimum) are no longer displayed in the column header, but within the cells of the respective column. This helps to optimize the display especially when search or filter operations are combined with mathematical operations:

Support for disabled row selectors

In the HS Grid (see also the ALV documentation) it is technically possible to disable the selection of individual rows. This functionality was not available in SAP GUI for Windows until now, but with release 8.10 we are introducing this feature. The following screenshot illustrates how the feature can look like based on a sample report:

The selection of the rows with the IDs 2 and 4 is disabled, but row 4 is pre-selected by the application.

Browser Control based on Edge (WebView2)

New pages in SAP GUI Options Dialog for Browser Control

The most important options affecting the Browser Control based on Edge (WebView2) are now available in SAP GUI options dialog. This also includes the settings for the features mentioned below.
New pages in options dialog:

  • Interaction Design > Browser Control
    On this page, the general setting which Browser Control is to be used can be configured. If Edge (based on Chromium) is used, two groups of other options are available:
    • General options (among others for SSO handling)
    • Options for handling downloads triggered via the HTML Control

  • Local Data > Edge Browser Control
    On this page, all settings related data retention of files downloaded via the Browser Control can be configured. The Browser Control based on Edge uses two download locations: One for downloaded files and one for temporary files:

Improved outplace SSO via Browser Control based on Edge, also with default browser

ABAP applications open external browser windows via HTML control method DETACH_URL_IN_BROWSER. In many cases, it is desired to use SSO for the application running in the newly created browser window, so that the user does not have to provide credentials again. In the past, this was only possible via the Edge browser extension “SAP GUI Connector for Microsoft Edge”. Now, with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 a new feature is available which allows the usage of SSO in this scenario without the need for installing and managing the browser extension. Furthermore, this feature allows the usage of the default browser instead of Microsoft Edge for the external browser windows. This means that while users still use the Browser Control based on Edge when working in SAP GUI for Windows, they do not have to use multiple different browsers when ABAP applications open URLs outplace of SAP GUI for Windows.

A minimum requirement for this feature is SAP_BASIS release 7.02. For older SAP Systems automatically SSO via a WebView2 window is activated. The mode can be configured via SAP GUI options dialog (see above).

Handling of downloads triggered via Browser Control based on Edge

The Browser Control based on Edge creates data in dedicated SAP GUI specific folders on the hard disk:

  • Files downloaded
  • Temporary data (cache)

Objects in these folders can be automatically cleared when SAP Logon is closed, the Browser Control is closed or after a certain period. This is important if downloaded files contain sensitive information and should not remain on the disk. Configuration is possible via a new page in SAP GUI options dialog (see above).
Furthermore, you can influence the behavior of downloads by using one of the newly introduced options:

  • Download files into the general downloads folder instead of the SAP GUI specific downloads folder
  • Demand a user confirmation for any file that is downloaded
  • Automatically launch files with trusted file types that have been downloaded via the Browser Control based on Edge. An example for this would be SAP Shortcut files (extension .sap)

Resizable Simple Browse window

ABAP applications can use method CL_GUI_HTML_VIEWER=>ENABLE_SIMPLEBROWSING to display URLs opened from within an HTML Control in a simple SAP GUI styled dialog box. Until now this dialog box had a fixed size and could not be resized. As of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 the WebView2 based HTML Control creates a resizable dialog box that better suits requirements of embedded documents:

Searchhelp control (amodal F4): Support for input history

Due to technical differences between the searchhelp control and the rest of SAP GUI, so far, input history was not supported for this component. This reduced usability, because often users keep entering similar values into search fields. This process can be substantially accelerated via an input history.

The input history in searchhelp control available as of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 supports almost the same feature set the input history for regular fields in SAP GUI for Windows does:

  • Entries are stored in the same history database
  • Entries are offered in a history dropdown if they match the present field content while typing
  • Entries can be taken over from the history dropdown into the field
  • History can be invoked via BACKSPACE / ALT + DOWN in an empty field (keyboard support is the same)
  • Entries can be removed from the history dropdown
  • The settings from SAP GUI Options dialog are respected (lifecycle, enabled for fields of a certain length only, etc.)
  • Input history can be activated or deactivated for individual fields via extended context menu
  • Input history can be deactivated by the administrator for individual fields
  • Input history can be completely deactivated in cases when the feature is not desired

More information can be found in SAP Note 3052911.

Table Control: Select All Visible Rows functionality

In SAP GUI for Windows 7.50 a “Select All Rows” functionality was available in Table Control. Since this functionality is limited and can only select the visible rows, it led to some confusion for users. However, there are users who liked the functionality regardless of its limitations. Therefore, as of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10, a registry value can be set that activates a new “Select All Visible Rows” button in case the respective Table Control has been configured to allow the selection of multiple rows by the ABAP application. For more information, see SAP Note 2576491.

Keyboard Support

Keyboard Improvements in various components

Several keyboard commands have been added to further improve usability for users working intensively with the keyboard:

  • Menu: HOME moves the selection to the topmost element on the current menu level
  • Menu: END moves the selection to the bottommost element on the current menu level
  • Menu: SPACE activates the currently selected element (in addition to the already supported ENTER)
  • Table Control: CURSOR DOWN scrolls line-wise down when the focus is  in the last visible row
  • Table Control: CURSOR UP scrolls line-wise up when the focus is on a cell in the first visible row
  • Main window: CTRL + ALT + S and CTRL + ALT + T set the focus back to the element the focus came from if activated a second time
  • Options dialog: The SAP GUI options dialog can be invoked via CTRL + , from the SAP GUI window as well as SAP Logon
  • Header and footer toolbars: HOME moves the focus to the first button in the toolbar
  • Header and footer toolbars: END moves the focus to the last button in the toolbar
  • Header and footer toolbars: CURSOR UP and CURSOR DOWN can be used as alternatives to CURSOR LEFT and CURSOR RIGHT
  • Column Tree Control: Optionally, a navigation from one cell to the next via CURSOR keys can be activated. As opposed to TAB and SHIFT+TAB this kind of navigation does not change the selection, but only the focus when the feature is activated. This is especially useful when a user needs to explore the screen by navigating (for example when using a Screenreader software). See SAP Note 3420682 for more information.
  • Column Tree Control: CTRL + HOME and CTRL + END have been harmonized with other UI elements in SAP GUI for Windows and now move the focus to the top left element / bottom right element.
  • Column Tree Control: CTRL + ALT + CURSOR KEYS can be used to move the focus within the grid area as well as the header area.
  • Searchhelp Control (amodal F4 help): The amodal searchhelp window is now fully integrated into the tab chain in SAP GUI for Windows. You can reach open searchhelp windows via group tabbing (CTRL + TAB / SHIFT + CTRL + TAB) and leave them the same way.
  • The decimal separator key on a numeric keypad can now be influenced by the “Decimal Notation” setting in the SAP system you are working in. A new option on the “Keyboard” page in SAP GUI options dialog allows the SAP system to define which character is inserted when the decimal character key on a numeric keypad is used. This feature is available for numerical input fields (also in Table Controls, but not in editable ALV Grids for technical reasons).
  • Copy and paste operations on input fields can now be triggered from the context menu as well.

Keyboard Support for reordering and resizing columns in tables

So far, resizing and reordering actions on columns in table structures were not easily possible via keyboard. As of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 the ALV Grid, the Table Control and the Column Tree Control support the following keyboard commands:

  • TAB / SHIFT + TAB: Move the focus into the column header via the regular tabchain and navigate within the column header area
  • CTRL + TAB / CTRL + SHIFT + TAB: Move the focus into the column header via the group tabchain
  • CTRL + ALT + CURSOR UP moves the focus into the column header when the focus is on a cell in the first row
  • CTRL + CURSOR LEFT: Move the currently focused column to the left by one position (only if application supports this)
  • CTRL + CURSOR RIGHT: Move the currently focused column to the right by one position (only if application supports this)
  • ALT + CURSOR LEFT: Reduce the width of the currently focused column by roughly one character. If you keep pressing ALT + CURSOR LEFT, the column continues to be resized
  • ALT + CURSOR RIGHT: Increase the width of the currently focused column by roughly one character. If you keep pressing ALT + CURSOR RIGHT, the column continues to be resized

In Table Control, the initial focus position remains to be on the first editable cell to ensure compatibility with the previous behavior. However, you can use the option explained in SAP Note 3558512 to set the initial focus position into the column header of a Table Control instead.

In addition to the extensions above, the Table Control has been extended to also allow an easier selection of columns via keyboard (if the application permits multiple selection for columns).

While the focus is on a column header:

  • SHIFT + CURSOR LEFT: Selects the currently focused column and the one to the left. The focus is moved to the column to the left
  • SHIFT + CURSOR RIGHT: Selects the currently focused column and the one to the right. The focus is moved to the column to the right
  • If you would like to reduce the selection, you can do this via SPACE while the respective column is selected

SAP GUI Scripting: Support for .js files in the Script Recording Dialog

VBScript (extension .vbs) is the main scripting language used by SAP GUI for Windows to record scripts for automating applications run via SAP GUI for Windows. However, VBScript is being deprecated by Microsoft and will be gradually phased out from Windows deliveries. While there is presently still the option to activate VBScript, this option will certainly disappear in the future. The deprecation of this scripting language obviously introduces the need for an alternative scripting language used for recorded scripts in SAP GUI for Windows.

While older SAP GUI for Windows versions could already replay JScript (.js) scripts, they could not record them. This missing piece has been added in SAP GUI for Windows 8.10: You can now record and replay both JScript as well as VBScript files (for as long as VBScript remains to be available).

Integration into SAP Business Client

Support for SAP Shortcut types “Report” and “System Command”

SAP Shortcuts are typically used to launch a specified transaction. This kind of SAP Shortcut has always been working when used from SAP Business Client. However, there are two additional types of SAP Shortcuts (“Report” and “System Command”) which could not be used from SAP Business Client prior to release 8.10.

Now, all types of SAP Shortcuts can be used for SAP GUI embedded in SAP Business Client (both components require at least release 8.10):

  • Type “Transaction” for starting a transaction
  • Type “Report” for starting a report which would normally be launched via transactions SE38 / SA38
  • Type “System Command” for starting a system command like /H

SAP Note 103019 contains more information on the parameters available in SAP Shortcuts.

Collection of Support Information

Sometimes support situations are quite complex and different pieces of information are requested by SAP Support to create a clear picture of the issue and potential causes. Until now, these pieces of information had to be collected manually and often in multiple steps causing several iterations with SAP Support. Obviously, this causes delays which could easily be avoided.

SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 introduces a “Generate Support Information” functionality which can be triggered from the page “System Information” in SAP GUI options dialog. When triggered, the feature collects the following pieces of information:

  • Local SAP UI Landscape File containing information on connections and other objects added in SAP Logon by the user
  • Cached central SAP UI Landscape Files containing information on connections and other objects added in SAP Logon by the administrator
  • If available, the database containing information on an adjusted local tab order
  • Trace files from the SAP GUI traces folder
  • Information on the DPI settings of used display devices
  • Information on environment variables which have been defined
  • Information on regional settings
  • Information on the version of the installed WebView2 browser control
  • Information on the files loaded by SAP GUI for Windows
  • An export of the Windows registry for the SOFTWARE\SAP\ branches under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE and HKEY_CURRENT_USER
  • A collection of registry values not directly belonging to SAP GUI for Windows, but possibly having impact on SAP GUI for Windows (like for the used version of Microsoft Office)

All of this is put into an archive which can be attached to a support incident at SAP. Together with a good description of the concrete issue including screenshots and a step-by-step set of instructions on how to reproduce the issue this helps to speed up the analysis.

Changed Features in Release 8.10

Focus: Animated focus is no longer default as of Belize themes

For more than 10 years the animated focus was the default setting for all SAP GUI themes except Classic Theme which never supported the animated focus. The animated focus provided a superior visualization compared to the classic “dotted focus”.

Nowadays with the introduction of improvements to the dotted focus starting with Belize themes, the animated focus does not have this advantage anymore. Especially with the Horizon themes the visualization of the focused element is very strong (see also the image below). Therefore, and since the dotted focus consumes much less resources, we decided on making the dotted focus the default as of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10.

If you prefer the animated focus, you can of course restore it by setting the respective registry value (see also the SAP GUI Administration Guide).

OK Code Field: Enhanced Search is activated by default

In SAP GUI for Windows 8.00 the enhanced search for transaction codes in the OK code field / command field was introduced (see also SAP Note 3075781). Since this feature was well received and boost user productivity, it is now activated by default if the technical prerequisites regarding your SAP system are met:

Here a simple example for a match list when the user searched for the term “system messages”:

Copy & Paste: TAB separated Copy & Paste is activated by default

Via CTRL + Y, SAP GUI for Windows allows copy & paste operations on multiple fields with a single action. Traditionally, contents belonging to different objects were separated by blanks / spaces. However, in SAP GUI for Windows 7.70 a new mode for copy & paste operations was introduced as an optional feature. With this feature, content belonging to multiple fields is separated by TAB characters when copied into the clipboard. Likewise pasting expects TAB characters instead of blanks, too. This is for example helpful when interacting with content from Microsoft Excel or other applications using TAB as a separator. See SAP Note 2796898 for more information.

As of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10, TAB separated Copy & Paste is the default mode for operations driven via CTRL + Y. If needed, the old default can be restored via options dialog:

Input History: Default value for maximum field length increased to 100

The input history in SAP GUI for Windows is only available for fields up to a certain length in characters. This is to avoid storing history information for texts rather than individual pieces of data. In the past the default length was set to 59 characters, but there are more and more applications using longer fields for data like an address. Therefore, the new default value for this parameter is 100.

Removed Components in Release 8.10

Business Explorer (SAP BI Frontend) is no longer integrated

The component “Business Explorer” containing the Frontend Add-On for SAP Business Intelligence (BI) has been removed from the delivery of SAP GUI for Windows 8.10. See SAP Note 3691456 for more information.
The component is henceforth delivered individually, but can be added to an existing NWSAPSetup installation server to combine it together with SAP GUI for Windows into a single installation package.

Final Thoughts on 8.10

From my perspective, 8.10 adds several long-expected features (Horizon themes, Custom Theming) while it also adds very useful enhancements for all types of users (the administrator will like the caching of configuration files), the power users will like the keyboard handling, everybody will love the changes in HTML Control based on Edge. Overall it will make life for many users just a bit more pleasant.

And finally, 8.10 opens the way for future innovation by establishing a connection between SAP applications running in SAP GUI for Windows with SAP Joule via SAP Business Client. Especially this may be interesting for many companies, because our experience shows that manual processes can be made substantially more efficient via this approach.

Kind regards,
Frank Krause
Development Manager SAP GUI for Windows
SAP Development

entry.title: Re: Automatic Alphanumeric Internal Number Assignment for...
entry.content:
Dear @Adesh_SAP1,

Based on the current SAP standard behavior, automatic alphanumeric internal number assignment in a predefined format such as EQP000001 or SER000001 is not supported as standard number range configuration in Public Cloud

Question 1.

For Equipment number ranges are maintained through Define Number Ranges for Equipment Categories / SSCUI 103091, but standard internal assignment is based on numeric number range intervals. SAP does not provide a standard prefix-based format mask such as EQP + sequential number for internal Equipment numbering

More details below:

SAP Help / Configuration Activity – Define Number Ranges for Equipment Categories

For Serial Numbers, standard configuration also does not provide a simple prefix-based internal number range format. However, SAP provides released extensibility options for custom serial number creation. More details you will find in answer to Question 2

Question 2.

For Equipment, no standard SSCUI, CBC setting or released BAdI was identified that changes standard internal Equipment number assignment into an automatic alphanumeric prefix format. SSCUI 103091 only controls equipment category number range assignment but it does not provide prefix logic

For Serial Numbers, standard configuration alone is not sufficient, but SAP provides released extensibility options, including LOSN_CBO_SERIAL_NUMBER_INT – Internal Serial Number Assignment using Custom Business Objects and LOSN_SER_NUM_INT_CREATE – Internal Serial Number Assignment using Developer Extensibility. These options should be evaluated first if the requirement is to generate serial numbers automatically according to a customer-specific pattern

More details below:

SAP Help – Extensibility for Serial Number Creation
SAP Community Blog – Implement Custom Serial Numbers with an Internal Number Range Using Developer Extensibility

As supporting evidence for standard Cloud number range behavior, SAP KBA 3676455 confirms that predefined alphanumeric internal number generation is not supported for standard internal number ranges, because interval limits must be numeric

More details below:

SAP KBA 3676455 – Automatic material number generation using predefined format in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Question 3.

No public SAP roadmap confirms planned standard support for automatic alphanumeric internal Equipment or Serial Number number ranges

For Equipment, if a controlled alphanumeric format is mandatory the recommended approach is an alternative process such as controlled external assignment through integration/side-by-side extension or submitting an SAP improvement request

For Serial Numbers, first recommended step is to evaluate the released SAP serial number extensibility options. SAP BTP or another side-by-side extension should be considered only if the required governance, counter logic, cross-object synchronization or integration process cannot be covered safely with released SAP extensibility

More details below:

SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Best Practices & End-to-End Configuration for Alternative Investment Funds (AIF) in SAP TRM
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are currently working on a requirement to map and configure Alternative Investment Funds (AIF) within SAP Treasury and Risk Management (TRM).

Since there is no dedicated standard product category out-of-the-box for AIFs, we are evaluating the most robust way to design this end-to-end, from master data setup to accounting postings and periodic valuation.

### Our Scenario & Dilemma

AIFs operate a bit differently than traditional mutual funds or bonds due to irregular capital calls, variable drawdowns, and distribution structures. We are currently weighing two primary approaches in the Securities (Transaction Manager) area:

 1. Product Category 020 (Investment Certificates): This seems ideal if the AIF is unitized and we receive a regular Net Asset Value (NAV) update.

 2. Product Category 160 (Participations / Private Equity): This looks like a better fit for handling manual capital drawdowns and distributions where tracking a fixed number of units isn't the primary driver.

### Where We Need Guidance

I would highly appreciate it if anyone who has implemented AIFs or Private Equity structures in TRM could share their insights, standard configuration steps, or documentation on the following:

 Product & Transaction Types: Which product category (020 vs 160) did you choose for your AIF implementation, and what were the key deciding factors?

 Flow Types & Conditions: How do you standardly handle subsequent capital calls (drawdowns) and profit distributions without messing up the original position?

Position Management & Accounting:  What Position Management Procedure (PMP) steps do you recommend for the periodic fair-value valuation of these funds?

 Data Migration: If you transitioned existing AIF investments into TRM from a legacy system or another module (like Asset Accounting), what uploader tools or best practices did you use to bring in historical positions?

If anyone has an end-to-end configuration blueprint, a step-by-step guide, or key lessons learned from a similar rollout, please share your thoughts!

Thanks in advance for your time and expertise.

entry.title: Re: SAP SEGW vs RAP vs CAP: Making the most of Function Imports, Actions and Functions (Part 2)
entry.content: Are there any use cases where you would still recommend SEGW over RAP in an on-premise/private cloud environment for OData generation? In other words, is there anything that SEGW can do but RAP cannot. This is crucial to select the most suitable path and avoid rework down the line.

entry.title: how to read SAP Application Message Type in integration
entry.content:
Hello all,

entry.title: Known Limitation in Timesheet Approval Center – "Approve All" Button
entry.content:
We enabled the Timesheet Approval Center for one of our clients and, during validation, identified that the "Approve All" button remains enabled when "All Status" is selected, irrespective of the actual timesheet statuses. In contrast, the functionality behaves as expected when a specific status is selected.

 

I highlighted this behavior to the SAP team for review. After investigating the issue, SAP confirmed that this is a product limitation rather than a configuration issue. Based on the issue we raised, SAP has now published a Knowledge Base Article (KBA) documenting this limitation.

https://me.sap.com/notes/3776833

This confirms that the observed behavior is an existing product limitation and not an issue specific to configuration.

entry.title: Re: Asset Year change
entry.content:
As the screenshot is unclear, I'm unsure about the exact nature of the dump you are experiencing. I hope the attached KBA can help identify your dump and assist in resolving your issue.

KBA 3366449 -  Dump or memory issue occurred in transaction FAGLGVTR or AFAB.
- check whether AFAR is performed before FAGLGVTR.

As mentioned in above KBA : "Depreciation recalculation (T-code: AFAR) is a must step before running depreciation posting(T-code: AFAB) and balance carry forward(T-code: FAGLGVTR), since depreciation recalculation (T-code: AFAR) is a critical component of the balance carryforward process and generates updates upfront."

entry.title: Re: Clarification Required on AUC Asset Value Display Bef...
entry.content:
Down payment postings are processed independently from acquisition postings, and the clearing of down payments is conducted separately from value transfer or settlement activities.

In this instance, there are no duplicate entries involved. The observed system behavior is correct.

Kindly check also the following documentation in connection with down payment:

Down Payments to Assets Under Construction

entry.title: Re: Posting not possible (Transaction type not allowed in...
entry.content:
Kindly refer to the following Note in connection with this issue:

Note 3130895 - AIBU: Settlement of retirement with revenue leads to follow-on problems.

This note explains that posting a retirement with revenue to an independent AuC with line item management is not a program error, but it is no longer allowed because it can cause follow-on issues during settlement and reporting.
In these cases, the system stops the posting with message AA359 “Posting is not possible (transaction type not allowed for class &).”

From a business process perspective, the retirement with revenue should be recorded on the completed/final asset rather than on the AuC. The recommended process is therefore:
1- Settle the AuC to the completed asset.
2- Post the retirement with revenue on the completed asset.

If a retirement with revenue was already posted on the AuC and then settled, the settlement must first be reversed. After that, the retirement with revenue on the AuC can be reversed.

entry.title: Restrict Project Creation in CJ20N to Project Templates Only and Prevent WBS Structure Changes After
entry.content:
Hi SAP Experts,

I have a business requirement in SAP Project System (PS), and I would appreciate your advice on the best standard solution or enhancement approach.

Requirement:

  1. Users should only be allowed to create new projects in CJ20N by copying from a predefined Project Template.
  2. Creating a project from scratch should not be allowed.
  3. After the project is copied from the template, users should be able to maintain project data (dates, responsible persons, etc.), but they should not be allowed to:
    • Add new WBS elements.
    • Delete existing WBS elements.
    • Change the project hierarchy/structure.

The objective is to ensure that every operational project follows the exact WBS structure defined in the project template.

entry.title: Re: Issues with CWIP line item
entry.content:
The error message "You cannot post to this fixed asset; Fiscal year already closed" (Message No. AA370) is usually triggered because the relevant fiscal year for the posting is closed in Asset Accounting.

This error (AA370) appears, for example, in transaction KO88 when attempting to perform a settlement for a closed fiscal year. The system is functioning as designed by preventing postings to closed fiscal years.

If the fiscal year cannot be reopened, it is not possible to post or settle in that year. However, you can settle costs from previous fiscal years into the current open fiscal year by following the guidance provided in SAP Note 425601.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Stop Asset Depreciation Effective Mid-Year...
entry.content:
For error AIST007, please refer to SAP Note 1113840 - Check of time-dependent depreciation terms. It states, "A new interval for time-dependent depreciation terms can only be created for the first day of a period."

If shutdown is an option you can consider, please refer to the following KBAs:

  • KBA 2163624: Asset continues to depreciate after setting SHUTDOWN
  • KBA 2444093: How to "shutdown" an asset?

entry.title: Re: Expired Useful Life” and “Expired Periods”
entry.content:
The SAP standard design is that the system calculates the expired useful life from depreciation start date which is in a closed fiscal year, until the last period of last closed fiscal year.

For more information on this system behavior kindly refer to the following SAP Notes:

19264 - Expired useful life in the asset master record

2667137 - Display design of field "Exp. Useful Life" in asset master
