entry.title: Internal Manage Early Numbering
entry.content:
METHOD earlynumbering_create.
    "Declare Variable First
    DATA: number_range_key               TYPE cl_numberrange_runtime=>nr_number,
          number_range_return_code       TYPE cl_numberrange_runtime=>nr_returncode,
          number_range_returned_quantity TYPE cl_numberrange_runtime=>nr_quantity.

    LOOP AT entities into DATA(ls_entity).
        "Try Calling The Method First
        TRY.
            cl_numberrange_runtime=>number_get(
              EXPORTING
                nr_range_nr       = '01'
                object            = 'ZCLA'
                quantity          = 1
                toyear            = ls_entity-Fiscalyear
              IMPORTING
                number            = number_range_key
                returncode        = number_range_return_code
                returned_quantity = number_range_returned_quantity
            ).
          "Catch the error into the message
          CATCH cx_nr_object_not_found INTO DATA(lx_not_found).
              APPEND VALUE #(  %cid      = ls_entity-%cid
                               %key      = ls_entity-%key
                               %msg      = lx_not_found
                            ) TO reported-zi_cla_exp_header.
              APPEND VALUE #(  %cid      = ls_entity-%cid
                               %key      = ls_entity-%key
                            ) TO failed-zi_cla_exp_header.
            RETURN.
          CATCH cx_number_ranges INTO DATA(lx_ranges).
            APPEND VALUE #(  %cid      = ls_entity-%cid
                               %key      = ls_entity-%key
                               %msg      = lx_ranges
                            ) TO reported-zi_cla_exp_header.
              APPEND VALUE #(  %cid      = ls_entity-%cid
                               %key      = ls_entity-%key
                            ) TO failed-zi_cla_exp_header.
            RETURN.
        ENDTRY.
        ls_entity-NumClaim = number_range_key+14(6).
        "Append value to the table
        APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = ls_entity-%cid
                        %key      = ls_entity-%key
                      ) TO mapped-zi_cla_exp_header.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

 

I have created  an early numbering implementation method like this. But it seems when I want to create data, it always output an error like below

I've already tried manual numbering insert by hard code it in the method. What should i do? Is there anyway i can check the append to the mapped-zi_cla_exp_header.?

I will attached the table definition also 

@EndUserText.label : 'Custom Table for Claim Expense Header'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zcla_exp_h {

  key client         : abap.clnt not null;
  key num_claim      : abap.numc(6) not null;
  name               : abap.char(64);
  description        : abap.char(128);
  datefrom           : abap.dats;
  dateto             : abap.dats;
  fiscalyear         : gjahr;
  companycode        : bukrs;
  costcenter         : kostl;
  created_by         : abp_creation_user;
  created_at         : abp_creation_tstmpl;
  last_changed_by    : abp_lastchange_user;
  last_changed_at    : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;

}
@EndUserText.label : 'Custom Table for Claim Expense Item'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zcla_exp_item {

  key client         : abap.clnt not null;
  key num_claim      : abap.numc(6) not null;
  key fiscalyear     : gjahr not null;
  key item_num       : abap.numc(5) not null;
  date_post          : abap.dats;
  event_description  : abap.char(128);
  category           : abap.char(64);
  currency           : waers;
  amount             : abap.dec(23,2);
  local_last_changed : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;

}

Here is the behavior definition also 

managed implementation in class zbp_i_cla_exp_header unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_CLA_EXP_HEADER //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zcla_exp_h
lock master
authorization master ( instance )
early numbering
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create ( authorization : global );
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) NumClaim;
  association _items { create; }
}

define behavior for ZI_CLA_EXP_ITEM //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zcla_exp_item
lock dependent by _header
authorization dependent by _header
//etag master <field_name>
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) NumClaim;
  association _header;
}

 

Thank you for the attention

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

entry.title: SAP Fiori Tools 2607 Update: Skills, Fullstack AI Development, and Performance
entry.content:
The 2607 release of SAP Fiori tools focuses on AI-driven development, but there is also good news for the human tooling: faster startup, more Page Editor capabilities, and a few quality-of-life fixes. Here is what is new.

AI-Driven Development with the MCP Server for SAP Fiori

MCP support in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio now includes an AI Chat capability that you can use for MCP-supported AI-driven SAP Fiori development directly from the browser. No installation is needed: the MCP server for SAP Fiori comes pre-installed in SAP Fiori and Fullstack Dev spaces, alongside the UI5 and CAP MCP servers.

Installing the MCP server locally

For local development, the MCP server for SAP Fiori is now available through the MCP registry on GitHub. You can also install it as a Claude plugin, together with our new skills (more on that below).

By the way, if you have the SAP Fiori tools extension pack installed in VS Code, you automatically have a version of the MCP server for SAP Fiori available that can be consumed by the GitHub Copilot extension.

Skills: the next layer on top of MCP

MCP servers provide the tools an AI agent can call, but tools alone do not tell the agent how to approach a complex, multi-step development job. That is the job of a skill. A skill bundles domain knowledge, step-by-step guidance, and best practices into a reusable prompt-based workflow that directs the AI through a specific task. Instead of leaving the model to figure out the right sequence of tool calls on its own, a skill encodes that knowledge explicitly, leading to more reliable and consistent results.

With this release, the SAP Fiori tools team publishes the first set of skills that complement the MCP server for SAP Fiori:

SkillWhat it does
sap-fiori-app-developmentGuides the creation and modification of SAP Fiori elements applications in CAP and standalone projects
sap-fiori-analytical-chartAdds an analytical chart and table hybrid to a List Report, supporting CAP and ABAP RAP (OData V4)
sap-fiori-add-visual-filterAdds chart-based visual filters to the filter bar or value help in SAP Fiori elements applications
sap-fiori-create-cliRuns and automates the @​sap-ux/create CLI for generating, converting, and managing Fiori projects
sap-fiori-eslint-pluginConfigures, migrates, or runs ESLint with the @​sap-ux/eslint-plugin-fiori-tools in SAP Fiori projects

The easiest way to get the skills is through the Claude plugin, where they are included alongside the MCP server for SAP Fiori. Alternatively, you can install individual skills via skills.sh, an open ecosystem for reusable agent skills that supports Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and others.

We will continuously expand and improve this set of skills, and we are very much looking forward to your feedback.

Fullstack development in VS Code

The MCP server for SAP Fiori pairs with the new ABAP MCP server, which is part of the ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code. Together, the two bridge the gap between backend ABAP services and the SAP Fiori frontend, enabling a true fullstack AI-driven development experience from within VS Code. For a concrete walkthrough of how this can look in practice, I recommend watching my ABAPConf presentation.

A new top-level tool structure

Based on feedback we have collected from usage, we restructured the top-level tools of the MCP server for SAP Fiori. More capabilities are now surfaced directly at the top level, in particular the app generators and metadata retrieval, which are needed to properly connect backend services to the frontend layer. This restructuring is available as of version 1.8.1 of the MCP server for SAP Fiori.

The top-level tools now are:

ToolWhat it does
search_docsSearches SAP Fiori elements, annotations, UI5, OPA5, and SAP Fiori tools documentation
list_fiori_appsScans a directory to find existing SAP Fiori applications
list_sap_systemsLists SAP systems stored in the user's environment
download_odata_service_metadataDownloads the EDMX metadata of an OData service from an SAP system
generate_fiori_app_odataGenerates a new SAP Fiori application for OData (non-CAP) projects, for example RAP-based services
generate_fiori_app_capGenerates a new SAP Fiori application inside an existing CAP project
list_functionality (Step 1 of 3)Lists supported modification operations for an existing SAP Fiori application
get_functionality_details (Step 2 of 3)Returns the required parameters for a chosen modification
execute_functionality (Step 3 of 3)Executes the modification with the provided parameters

This structure also sets the stage for what comes next. We are working on adaptation project support for the MCP server for SAP Fiori, which will add tools for creating and modifying adaptation projects directly through AI agents. They will join the top-level structure shown above.

Renovations and Performance Improvements

Moving from CommonJS to ECMAScript Modules

ECMAScript Modules (ESM) are the official JavaScript module standard, supported natively in modern browsers and Node.js. With this release, the SAP Fiori tools suite and its Node.js modules migrate from CommonJS to ESM. For us, this means better ecosystem compatibility, tree-shaking for smaller bundles (unused code eliminated at build time), and more efficient asynchronous module loading.

ESLint 10 upgrade

The 2603 release delivered ESLint 9 support for the tools and generator. With 2607 we take the next step and upgrade to ESLint 10, keeping the toolchain current and continuing the move toward the modern flat config format.

80% faster extension startup

As the SAP Fiori tools extensions have grown over time, startup time had crept up correspondingly. Through targeted refactoring and more efficient bundling, loading only what is necessary at startup, we reduced the startup time of the SAP Fiori tools extensions by around 80%.

Other Improvements

A few smaller additions that are worth calling out:

  • Fiori Generator error details: Clicking on an error in the generator now opens the full error details in the console, making it much easier to diagnose what went wrong.
  • Page Editor building blocks: The Page Editor now supports Page, Form, and Field building blocks in custom pages and custom sections, rounding out the building block coverage for custom development.
  • SAP Fiori elements enhancements via Page Editor: The Page Editor now lets you configure a notes reuse component, inline edit, mass edit, and creation dialogs (and more) for SAP Fiori elements applications, all through the graphical configuration interface without touching annotations manually.
  • Reuse existing LineItem annotations for tables: When adding a table, you can now select an existing LineItem annotation from the service instead of always having to create a new one. This avoids duplicate annotations and makes it easier to build UIs on top of well-annotated services.
  • Unique control IDs in XML generation: The Page Editor now correctly generates unique control IDs when producing XML for custom elements.
  • Download UI5 ABAP repository content from the public cloud: You can now download source artifacts directly from a deployed SAPUI5 ABAP repository, so you can continue local development even when the source files are not available. This closes a gap for public cloud customers, who previously had no way to import from the ABAP backend. See the SAP Help Portal documentation for more information.

Staying Up to Date

If you want to track changes in SAP Fiori tools between release blog posts, the What's New section in the SAP Help Portal is the best place to look. It is updated continuously with newly delivered capabilities and fixes.

For documentation and sample code covering all building blocks available when creating SAP Fiori elements applications, visit the SAP Fiori development portal.

Questions and feedback? Leave a comment below or reach out through the community.

entry.title: Re: Remaining value in the asset even after completion of...
entry.content:
In the majority of cases, when an asset is not fully depreciated at the end of its useful life, this is due to the depreciation start date being earlier than the acquisition date.

In this case manual correction is needed.

entry.title: Bank Statement Monitor – How is the Contact Information section populated
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am currently testing the Bank Statement Monitor app and I am trying to understand how the Contact Information section is populated.

In my scenario, I created a Bank Contact Person at the bank account level in Manage Bank Accounts and maintained all relevant contact details there, expecting this information to be displayed in the Bank Statement Monitor. However, the fields remain empty.

Specifically, the following fields are not populated:

  • Bank Contact Person
  • Bank Contact Person Phone Number

What I have checked so far:

  • The bank contact person is maintained at the bank account level.
  • The bank account is active and visible in Bank Statement Monitor.
  • Bank statements are processed successfully and displayed correctly in the app.
  • I checked table FAR_BSM_RESULT, but I could not find any contact-related information that would explain how these fields are derived.
  • I could not identify any configuration or master data dependency responsible for populating this section.

My questions are:

  1. What is the source of the Contact Information displayed in Bank Statement Monitor?
  2. Is it derived from the contact person maintained in Manage Bank Accounts, or from a different object/table?
  3. Are there any prerequisites, activation steps, jobs, CDS views, BAdIs, or configuration settings required for these fields to be populated?
  4. Has anyone successfully displayed contact information in this section and can share where the data comes from?

I have attached a screenshot showing the empty Contact Information section.

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance!

Best regards,
Laura

entry.title: Re: wrong WBS ID while creating project from template
entry.content:
After entering the operative coding mask and selecting the standard project template to copy from, click WBS Overview (as shown in Screenshot 1). This opens the WBS overview screen (Screenshot 2).

From there, use the Replace function to replace the WBS IDs with the correct project-specific values. This updates the WBS element IDs to match your new project coding.

If your requirement is for this to happen automatically during the copy process, you can implement enhancement CNEX0033. This enhancement allows you to influence the project copy process and programmatically replace the generated WBS IDs so that they follow your desired naming convention (for example, changing W10.CP00.WCC.CCCC to W10.CP00.W10.2603) without requiring manual search and replace after the copy.

entry.title: Re: Can the Tax Code be Edited in a Debit Memo Created wi...
entry.content:
Dear @hyik,

Question 1.

No. I am not aware of a standard configuration or standard business process in Public Cloud that allows the Tax Code to be manually overwritten in a Debit Memo / Debit Memo Request created with reference to a Sales Order. In the standard SD process the Tax Code is an attribute of the tax condition record for example TTX1 and is determined automatically via pricing, based on the departure/destination country and the tax classifications of the customer and the material. It is not a manually maintainable field in the Debit Memo

More details below:

SAP KBA 3308072 - Output tax determination in sales billing - SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Question 2.

The recommended SAP approach is to correct the tax-determining data and then redetermine pricing/taxes, instead of manually changing the Tax Code in the Debit Memo. This typically means checking the customer tax classification, material/product tax classification, tax condition records such as TTX1, tax rate validity and the relevant tax code configuration. If the billing document was already created with the wrong tax result the usual approach is to cancel/reverse it and recreate it after the tax setup has been corrected

More details below:

SAP KBA 2799312 – Pricing determination fails due to no valid condition record exists for the date which system uses to fetch the condition record

SAP KBA 3009061 – How to create tax code in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP Help – Tax Codes

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: OData Service field empty when creating Analytical List Page from Custom Analytical Query S/4 Public
entry.content:
System: SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition — Release [2602]

Context:

I built a commercial margin analysis report following the standard key user extensibility flow:

  1. Custom CDS View (Analytical Cube scenario) on top of I_SalesOrderItemCube, associated with I_SalesDocument, I_FiscalCalendarDate, I_CustomerSalesArea, and I_SalesDocItemPricingElement.

     

  2. Custom Analytical Query built on that view, with calculated measures (weighted %Margin) and several dimensions.

     

  3. The query is Published (green status) and works correctly in Preview and in the Query Browser / embedded SAC view — I validated the calculations extensively and everything matches.

The problem:

When trying to create a KPI or Analytical List Page artifact from the "Custom Analytical Queries and Services" app / View Browser, the "OData Service" field appears empty in the dropdown (both when searching by CDS View and when searching directly by OData Service), preventing me from completing the artifact creation. No service appears under "Recently Published InA Services" either (shows "No data"), even though the query itself shows as published.

My question:

Is there an additional explicit step required to generate the internal InA/OData service that "Manage KPIs and Reports" consumes from a Custom Analytical Query, or is this an expected limitation for this scenario (Analytical Cube + multiple associated data sources)? Has anyone successfully created a KPI or ALP from a query with this kind of configuration?

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: From AI Policy to Daily Behaviour – How OCM Makes Governance Real
entry.content:
Policies look good in board packs. Behaviour decides what happens in real life. If you want responsible AI, you have to design for the micro‑moments where people quietly choose what to do with an AI output.

 

The Governance–Behaviour Gap

Everywhere you look, AI governance activity is booming. New policies. Fresh ethics frameworks. Risk registers with whole new sections. Committees that didn’t exist a year ago.

On paper, it’s impressive.
In practice, something crucial is missing.

Responsible AI hinges on thousands of small decisions made by people who may never read those policy PDFs. The call‑centre agent deciding what to do with a recommendation that feels unfair. The sales manager trying to explain AI‑influenced pricing to a customer. The analyst choosing whether to trust a model that contradicts their experience.

Policies define intent. Behaviour delivers outcomes.

That gap between the governance deck and the Tuesday afternoon decision is where Organisational Change Management (OCM) earns its keep.

 

What OCM Actually Adds to AI Governance

Think of governance as answering “what do we want AI to do, and under what conditions?” OCM answers “how do we make that real in daily work?”

In practical terms, OCM teams:

  • Translate abstract principles into role‑specific clarity: which AI tools touch which decisions, and where human judgement is non‑negotiable.

  • Design clear escalation pathways: how to question outputs, log concerns, and route feedback to governance bodies that can act.

  • Embed responsible AI practices into training, workflows, performance expectations, and cultural norms.

  • Tailor guidance by role, so “responsible AI” looks different for a call‑centre agent, a finance analyst, and a product manager – because their reality is different.

The result is a translation layer between macro‑principles and micro‑decisions.

 

Start With Roles, Not With Tools

One common anti‑pattern is building governance entirely around the technology stack. The better starting point is roles.

For each role family, co‑design answers to a handful of pragmatic questions:

  • Which AI capabilities does this role interact with, directly or indirectly?

  • Which decisions are now AI‑assisted – and what does “assisted” actually mean here?

  • Where is the line between “AI suggestion” and “human decision”?

  • When must the human override, double‑check, or escalate?

  • How will responsible behaviour be recognised and rewarded?

From these conversations, you can create concise “role guardrail” sheets – one to two pages per role family – that spell out:

  • AI touchpoints in the workflow.

  • Decision boundaries and human‑in‑the‑loop expectations.

  • Escalation routes and accountability.

These guardrails are then embedded where work lives: procedures, playbooks, performance frameworks, micro‑learning, team huddles, and on‑screen nudges.

 

Governance Has to Live in Workflows

Policies live on intranets. Behaviour lives inside systems and processes.

To make governance real, you have to design it into the workflow:

  • Human checkpoints. Sensitive decisions require explicit human review and sign‑off, even when the AI is confident.

  • Contextual prompts. Systems ask users to confirm data appropriateness, consent, and compliance before proceeding.

  • Deviation flags. Recommendations that materially differ from historical patterns trigger review rather than silent acceptance.

  • In‑app guidance. Brief “what good looks like” examples and one‑click escalation options appear at the moment of decision.

These are design choices, not technical inevitabilities. OCM’s role is to work with process owners and architects so that these choices become standard, not exceptions.

 

Culture, Conversations, and Psychological Safety

Even beautifully designed workflows will fail if people don’t feel safe to question the AI.

Responsible AI depends on speak‑up cultures where:

  • Leaders talk openly about trade‑offs, boundaries, and uncertainties – not just innovation and speed.

  • Teams have psychologically safe spaces to surface discomfort or doubt about AI outputs.

  • Governance bodies hear front‑line signals promptly and act on them in visible ways.

OCM helps build this culture. It equips leaders with the language to discuss risk and ethics, designs forums where employees can share what they are seeing, and ensures there are clear routes from “this feels wrong” to “someone is investigating and will respond”.

 

Using SAP’s AI Ethics Commitments as an Anchor

If your organisation runs on SAP, you have a practical anchor for AI governance work: SAP’s published AI ethics principles and Business AI commitments.

They offer:

  • A ready‑made foundation for internal AI policies when SAP systems underpin your core processes.

  • A shared language for training and communication about SAP Business AI.

  • A bridge to regulators and partners, aligning internal governance with vendor‑level commitments.nasstar+1

OCM teams can:

  • Turn these principles into role‑specific guidance for people using SAP solutions.

  • Influence workflow design in SAP‑based processes so ethics are reflected in how work flows.

  • Build learning content that links “this is how SAP designed the AI” with “this is how we use it responsibly here”.

 

Making Governance Tangible: OCM Deliverables

To keep this grounded, here are concrete deliverables OCM can own:

  • Role guardrail sheets for priority role families.

  • Decision taxonomies with risk tiers and human‑in‑the‑loop patterns.

  • Escalation playbooks, intake forms, and response SLAs.

  • In‑app guidance copy and short micro‑learning capsules.

  • Behavioural dashboards showing escalations, oversight rates, sentiment, and policy‑related incidents.

These artefacts turn governance from concept into practice.

 

How You’ll Know It’s Working

Indicators that governance is moving from paper to behaviour include:

  • Healthy rates of AI‑related escalations with reasonable time‑to‑resolution.

  • High percentages of sensitive decisions with recorded human oversight.

  • Strong adherence to decision guardrails in audit samples.

  • Employee sentiment showing confidence to question AI and clarity on who owns decisions.

  • Fewer policy violations tied to AI use – and issues being caught earlier.

By contrast, watch for anti‑patterns:

  • Policy dumps without role tailoring.

  • Over‑reliance on training while workflows stay unchanged.

  • Fear of escalation and “don’t rock the boat” cultures.

  • Static governance that doesn’t adapt to frontline feedback.

OCM is the discipline that spots these patterns and helps fix them.

 

Turn Your AI Policy Into Behaviour

If your organisation has AI policies on paper but isn’t yet confident about day‑to‑day behaviour, start with one high‑impact process and one role family. Map the decisions, design the guardrails, build the escalation path – and let that become your template.

Call to action:
Invite your change and governance teams to co‑create a role‑based AI guardrail sheet for one critical role this quarter. Then use that as the model for the rest of the organisation.

 

See my last Blog at Why OCM Is the Missing Piece in Every Organisation... - SAP Community

entry.title: Re: equest for Standard Process to Transfer Amount from G...
entry.content:
Hi S0027020516,

Short answer: Yes, this is supported as standard. For upload-based posting to a supplier account against a G/L account, use the Import Supplier Invoices (F3041) app.

Is there standard functionality to transfer a balance from a G/L account to a supplier account?

Yes. The transfer is posted as an FI supplier invoice (or credit memo, depending on direction): the supplier line item carries the payable, and the offsetting line is the G/L account you want to relieve. For manual single postings, use the Create Incoming Invoices app. Reference: KBA 2885884 and SAP Help

Which standard app should be used for the upload process?

Import Supplier Invoices (F3041). You download a Microsoft Excel template to the local folder (or Google Sheets even to cloud), enter header data and the G/L account items, upload the file back to the app, check it for errors, and post the invoices — supporting mass processing in one go. 

Note that general journal entry apps (Post/Upload General Journal Entries) post to G/L accounts only, so they are not the right tool here — supplier postings go through the AP apps above. 

Is this a product limitation?

No. The scenario is fully supported as standard; no workaround is needed. Integration alternative: if the data originates in an external system, the SOAP API Journal Entry – Post supports supplier line items natively via the CreditorItem node. Reference: SAP Help

Constraints to verify:

  • F3041 does not support purchase-order-based invoices or one-time suppliers (see the SAP Help link above).
  • If the transfer requires debiting the supplier, use the credit memo transaction — check the Transaction field in your release's template.
  • If the source G/L account is open-item managed, clear it manually afterwards using the Clear G/L Accounts app, or ensure clearing occurs via the Automatic Clearing feature based on job scheduling.

Summary: Use Import Supplier Invoices (F3041) for the upload-based transfer (supplier line + G/L offset per document), or Create Incoming Invoices for individual manual postings. This is standard functionality, not a product limitation.

Hope it helps.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Request for Bulk Upload Facility in Post Outgoing Pay...
entry.content:
Hi S0027020516,

Short answer: No — Post Outgoing Payments (F1612) has no bulk/mass upload capability; it is by design a single-document app. The right bulk alternative depends on what you are actually posting in it — see the three scenarios below.

Is there any standard bulk upload functionality available for the Post Outgoing Payment app?

No. The app posts and clears a single outgoing payment in one step as declared on SAP Help; the documented assumption is that outgoing payments normally run via the payment program, and this app is only for immediate/manual exceptions. Reference: Post Outgoing Payments – SAP Help Portal

What is the recommended approach? (depends on what you post in the app)

Scenario 1 – You pay open supplier invoices: Use the Automatic Payment Program — Manage Automatic Payments (F0770), scope item J60. It selects due invoices, posts the clearing documents, and generates payment media in one run; proposals and payments can be scheduled. Reference: Manage Automatic Payments – SAP Help Portal

Scenario 2 – You post supplier against a bank/bank clearing account (recording payments executed outside the payment run): You can mass-post these with Import Supplier Invoices (F3041). The app imports multiple documents from a spreadsheet template (up to 2,000 entries per file), and the template's Transaction header field also supports credit memos — a supplier credit memo posts Debit supplier / Credit G/L, i.e. the same posting logic as a manual payment. References: Import Supplier Invoices – SAP Help Portal, Template header fields

Important: this creates accounting documents, not payment documents — the original invoices are not cleared by the upload. Make sure the characteristic fields like assignment are filled accordingly, so the items can be cleared afterwards via automatic clearing (Schedule Accounts Payable Jobs) or Clear Outgoing Payments. No payment media or payment advice is produced. Also note the created journal entry does not have characteristics as payment, but Invoice.

Scenario 3 – You post between the main bank account and bank subaccounts (pure G/L postings, e.g. transfers/clearing account entries): Use Upload General Journal Entries (F2548) — it mass-posts multiple journal entries from one spreadsheet. References: Upload General Journal Entries – SAP Help Portal

Is this a standard product limitation, and are there planned roadmap enhancements?

Yes, it is a standard design limitation — manual payment apps are intentionally single-document; mass processing is covered by the payment run and the import apps above.

Roadmap: As of today, no published roadmap item announces a bulk upload for Post Outgoing Payments. Check the SAP Road Map Explorer and consider raising/upvoting a request in SAP Customer Influence (Continuous Influence for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition).

Constraints to verify:

  • Scenario 2: whether your bank G/L account accepts direct manual postings — depending on your bank account architecture, manual postings typically go to bank subaccounts, with the main bank account posted via bank statement.
  • Scenario 2/3: neither upload clears supplier open items automatically; plan the clearing step.
  • Payment advices are only generated automatically from Manage Automatic Payments; for other postings, they must be created manually in Manage Payment Advices.

Summary: First clarify what the users actually post in Post Outgoing Payments. For paying open invoices, move to the Automatic Payment Program (J60). For recording externally executed supplier payments in bulk, use Import Supplier Invoices (credit memo logic) plus a clearing step; for pure bank/G/L postings, use Upload General Journal Entries. A bulk upload in F1612 itself doesn't exist.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Can I create intercompany AR/AP by post incoming invo...
entry.content:
Hi Gary,

Short answer: Yes, but there are three standard designs, and which one fits depends on whether you legally need invoices between the affiliates. The most automated standard options are 1GP (cross-company G/L posting with automatic intercompany clearing accounts — no AR/AP at all) and 4AN (automatic aggregated intercompany billing with one framework sales order per company pair — not one per transaction). Pure FI invoices without any order are also possible, but they are the most manual variant.

Option 1 – No legal invoice required (e.g., entities in one tax group): 1GP

Best fit if you don't need AR/AP at all. With Intercompany Financial Posting (1GP), you post one journal entry across both company codes; the system automatically splits it into one document per company code and generates the balancing lines on intercompany clearing accounts. Reference: Intercompany Posting in S4H Cloud – SAP blog

Constraint: No customer/supplier open items are created — the clearing accounts are the counterpart, so there is nothing to clear in the subledger; mutual balances sit on the clearing accounts. Clearing accounts are maintained in Automatic Account Determination → General Ledger → Prepare Intercompany Transactions.

Option 2 – Legal invoices required, high volume: intercompany cost allocations + 4AN

This is the standard automated solution for your volume concern. Cross-company CO postings (activity allocations, cost repostings) automatically offset via intercompany clearing accounts; a periodic billing job then aggregates them and creates the intercompany invoice (AR) in the delivering company, and the corresponding supplier invoice (AP) is posted automatically in the ordering company — classically via IDoc, and since release 2602 via the new bgPF feature "Automatically Post Payables for Classic Intercompany Invoices". References: Define Posting Scheme for Intercompany Clearing Accounts – SAP blog, KBA 2789808

Important: You only need one framework intercompany sales order per company pair, created once — not a sales order per transaction. The billing run aggregates all intercompany cost postings of the period against it. References: SAP Learning – 4AN, New Resource-Related Intercompany Billing – SAP blog

Constraint: 4AN bills intercompany cost allocation postings (CO), not arbitrary FI documents. If your daily transactions can be posted as cross-company cost allocations/repostings, this fits; if they are genuine trade transactions, it doesn't.

Option 3 – Legal invoices required, posted directly in FI (your original idea)

Yes, technically possible. Post the AR side via Create Outgoing Invoices / Upload Customer Open Items (SAP Help) and the AP side via Create Supplier Invoice without PO reference or Import Supplier Invoices (SAP Help), with the trading partner filled on the affiliate BPs. Each side is then cleared in its own company code (payment or Clear Open Items Automatically, SAP Help – Clearing Process).

Constraint: This is the least automated variant — no automatic counterpart posting, and verify country e-invoicing/DRC requirements for FI-only customer invoices.

Summary

First decide whether legal invoices are required between the affiliates. If not → 1GP cross-company postings with automatic clearing accounts (zero extra workload, no AR/AP). If yes and the transactions are internal cost transfers → 4AN with one framework intercompany sales order and fully automatic AR + AP posting. Only if neither fits, fall back to direct FI invoices with upload apps/APIs.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: What a mega yacht exhaust system can teach you about supplier marketing
entry.content:
Most supplier profiles are essentially a resume, and just like a resume, nobody reads them for fun. They scan for the relevant line and move on. What actually makes a buyer stop, read, and reach out is something closer to a story. 

Chris Luecke, host of the Manufacturing Happy Hour podcast, has spent years having candid conversations with manufacturing leaders about what's actually working in the industry and what isn't. In episode 3 of B2B Exchange, SAP's expert interview series for B2B suppliers, Chris makes a case that's simple but easy to overlook: companies that treat their digital presence as a storytelling channel, not just a directory listing, are the ones buyers remember and come back to. 

People remember stories 

People remember stories because they give us something to connect with. While selling manufacturing equipment, office supplies or packaging materials might seem like a purely transactional process, regardless of what you are selling, you are selling to people with shared human experiences. Chris's point isn't that you need to get emotional or creative for the sake of it, but rather that the claims suppliers make about their businesses (we're efficient, we're innovative, we create value) land differently when they're illustrated with a specific example instead of stated as fact. 

The art behind the metal 

In the interview, Chris shares how a manufacturer of exhaust systems for mega yachts hit a capacity bottleneck and looked at robotic welding as a solution. Not only did they catch up on orders, but they were also able to promote their existing welders into fabricators. Instead of just focusing on efficiency or cost savings from the automation, the manufacturer was able to show how they improved the lives of their employees leading to more skilled work and creativity, or more of “the art behind the metal” as Chris says.  

The story isn't about robots replacing workers, but rather the opposite, which is what makes it so compelling.  

What's the equivalent story in your business? What outcome have you produced for a customer that's worth more than a bullet point on your profile? 

Telling your story on SAP Business Network 

When Chris was exploring SAP Business Network, one feature caught his attention: the Company Showcase section of the Company Profile. It's where suppliers can go beyond the basics — certifications, service locations, catalog — and share the things that actually differentiate them: case studies, customer presentations, even a short video. Chris describes it as another channel where that story can be shared, and it's available through the SAP Business Network, promote subscription for suppliers who want to make the most of their presence on the network. 

Even before you get there, the free Company Profile is worth investing time into. Chris's point applies at every level: the more clearly your profile reflects who you are and what you actually do for customers, the better your chances of being the supplier your ideal customer reaches out to. 

Watch the new full episode 

Watch Grow Your Manufacturing Business for more of Chris's perspective on what it takes to stand out as a supplier today. And if you want to go deeper on how to make sure the right buyers are finding your profile in the first place, check out our conversation with Kyle Milan of Milan Media.  

Subscribe to the SAP YouTube channel to catch new B2B Exchange episodes as they drop, and follow the SAP Business Network for Suppliers tag on SAP Community to stay updated.

entry.title: SAC date variable input as MM/DD/YYYY?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

Business users want to enter values for a date variable in MM/DD/YYYY format in SAC story (built on Datasphere models) and I’m looking for guidance regarding date-variable input formatting.

Context:

  • Data is loaded from S/4HANA into the Datasphere Raw layer. Date fields initially arrive as strings in YYYYMMDD format (e.g., 20251231). We manually change the data type of date fields to the "Date".
  • In the Harmonization layer, we consume the Raw layer table without transformations.
  • In the Business layer, we apply cleansing, business logic, master data alignment, and assign semantics to date fields as Calendar Date.
  • In the Consumption layer, we build an Analytic Model and create variables on these date fields.
  • The SAC story is based on this Analytical Model, and the date variables appear as designed
  • The SAC user profile is configured to display dates in MM/DD/YYYY format.

Issue:

When running the story:
  • The variable's value help (possible values) shows the description in MM/DD/YYYY format and the ID displays in YYYY-MM-DD format. User can search in MM/DD/YYYY then select it and the variable show MM/DD/YYYY format (in form of description but accepts the entry as YYYY-MM-DD)
  • If the user manually enters a date as MM/DD/YYYY (without selecting from the value help), SAC throws an error: “Member ID does not exist”.
 

I understand that variables accept the key value rather than the description, but I want to know:

 

Is there any standard SAP-supported way for SAC variables to accept date input in MM/DD/YYYY format, without creating custom fields, casts, or transformations? Since we have many date fields, implementing custom logic repeatedly would be costly, so we’re hoping for a standard approach if one exists.

Thanks in advance for your insights!

Blogs referred:
Assistance Required – Date Format Handling in Data... - SAP Community

Solved: Date shown wrong in SAP Analytics Cloud based on S... - SAP Community

Regards,

Preet
